ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bento class (FOODON_00002809); no specific fried-ebi-and-beef-bento subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for fried ebi and beef bento. A00GH (composite rice dish) is the closest defensible anchor. Exact code unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific bento preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bento box serving of approximately 400 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice, stir-fried beef slices, and two pieces of ebi tempura.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Ebi and Beef Bento — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fried Ebi and Beef Bento (cr_id F05100057). All per-100g values are as reported. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy (beef stir-fry + shrimp tempura + white rice) and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried ebi and beef bento as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database for white rice, boiled, average). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 92.52 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 66.6, rounded to 66.9. The ebi tempura batter and stir-fried beef contribute minor additional carbohydrate but do not materially alter the GI estimate. This is a conservative analogous estimate; actual GI may vary with rice variety and cooking method.

hypertension.key_factor: Low per-100g sodium (93 mg) but high cumulative sodium risk from soy sauce, stir-fry seasoning, and large serving size; saturated fat from beef and deep-frying contributes to arterial stiffness.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and seasoning, limit the serving to half a bento box, and substitute stir-fried beef with a leaner protein to reduce saturated fat and cumulative sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a high GI (~72) and low dietary fibre (0.58 g/100g), producing rapid postprandial glucose elevation; tempura batter adds additional refined carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion by at least half, request brown rice if available, remove the tempura batter coating from the ebi, and pair the meal with non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates saturated and potentially trans fats; beef contributes saturated fat and haem iron; low dietary fibre (0.58 g/100g) provides minimal LDL-lowering benefit.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this meal to occasional consumption, request grilled or steamed shrimp in place of tempura, reduce the beef portion, and increase vegetable content to improve fibre intake and reduce saturated fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef contains arachidonic acid (omega-6) that amplifies inflammatory pathways; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are pro-inflammatory; white rice contributes to inflammatory glycaemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute beef with oily fish (e.g., salmon or mackerel), request grilled or steamed shrimp instead of tempura, and replace white rice with brown rice to reduce inflammatory glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Ebi and Beef Bento is a Japanese-style bento box meal sold widely in Singapore food courts and hawker centres. It consists of steamed white rice served alongside stir-fried sliced beef seasoned with soy sauce and two pieces of ebi tempura — whole prawns coated in a light wheat-flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crisp. The dish delivers a satisfying combination of textures: soft, slightly sticky rice; tender, savoury beef; and crunchy, juicy tempura prawns. Per 100 g, the dish provides 169 kcal, 7.1 g protein, 5.3 g fat, and 23.1 g carbohydrate, with low dietary fibre at 0.58 g and moderate sodium at 93 mg. The dominant carbohydrate source is white rice, which carries a high glycaemic index (~72), making this meal a high-glycaemic-load option unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance without portion modification. The deep-frying process introduces saturated fat and potentially pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). Clinically, this meal is moderate concern for hypertension due to cumulative sodium from seasoning, and moderate-to-high concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from beef and deep-frying. It is labelled halal and is a common working-lunch option in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is fried ebi and beef bento healthy?
How many calories in a beef and prawn tempura bento?
Is this bento suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of a Japanese bento with white rice?
Can I eat ebi tempura bento if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein is in a beef and shrimp bento box?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100057) with a reported confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous composite and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source, which is the standard defensible approach for composite rice dishes without a direct GI measurement.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100g) is not reported by HPB for this entry. It is estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogy: white rice (~0.1 g/100g sat fat) + stir-fried beef (~3.5 g/100g sat fat) + shrimp tempura (~1.8 g/100g sat fat), weighted by approximate component proportions (rice ~55%, beef ~25%, ebi tempura ~20%), yielding ~1.4 g/100g. This is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific composite bento dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. The input record listed 'low_sodium' as a dietary tag; this is retained cautiously as the per-100g sodium (93 mg) is low, but cumulative sodium from seasoning and soy sauce in a full serving may exceed low-sodium thresholds — this tag should be reviewed. 5. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
